The store-backed inbound Signal missing one-time-pre-key hardening slice added
after the no-one-time-pre-key receive work closes the codec-boundary failure
case where an inbound pre-key message references a local one-time pre-key that
has already been consumed or deleted. The high-level decrypt path now reuses
the already decoded pre-key envelope for session derivation instead of decoding
the same wire payload twice, and the new regression verifies the missing
pre-key error leaves no provider session or identity record behind. It was
verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_inbound_pre_key_when_one_time_pre_key_missing --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_inbound_pre_key_when_one_time_pre_key_missing --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_decrypts_inbound_pre_key_then_session_messages --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_decrypts_inbound_pre_key_then_session_messages --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_state_after_failed_pre_key_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_state_after_failed_pre_key_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The store-backed inbound Signal pre-key identity-change transaction slice added
after the missing one-time-pre-key hardening work closes the high-level codec
case where a valid inbound pre-key message decrypts successfully but carries a
sender identity that conflicts with already stored provider identity state. The
regression proves the provider identity transition rejects before consuming the
local one-time pre-key or overwriting the existing opaque provider session and
identity records. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_pre_key_after_inbound_identity_change --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_pre_key_after_inbound_identity_change --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_inbound_pre_key_when_one_time_pre_key_missing --quiet`

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_state_after_failed_pre_key_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_state_after_failed_pre_key_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The store-backed outbound Signal pre-key identity-change transaction slice
added after the inbound identity-change transaction work closes the first-send
case where a freshly injected key bundle conflicts with an existing provider
identity for that recipient. The high-level `encrypt_message` regression proves
the provider identity transition rejects before writing a new provider session
and preserves the existing provider identity record. It was verified in the
current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_outbound_pre_key_identity_change --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_outbound_pre_key_identity_change --quiet`

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_encrypts_direct_pre_key_without_one_time_pre_key --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_encrypts_direct_pre_key_without_one_time_pre_key --quiet`

- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`

The store-backed outbound Signal unusable-provider-session fallback slice added
after the outbound identity-change transaction work makes high-level
`encrypt_message` treat opaque/malformed provider session records and decodable
provider sessions without a paired provider identity as absent, allowing a
freshly injected pre-key session to recover first-send behavior. Real provider
identity mismatches still reject and preserve existing identity state. The
regression covers both an opaque provider session and a decodable provider
session missing identity, and verifies both are replaced by a fresh provider
session/identity from the injected key bundle. It was verified in the current
VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_uses_pre_key_when_existing_provider_session_is_unusable --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_uses_pre_key_when_existing_provider_session_is_unusable --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_outbound_pre_key_identity_change --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_outbound_pre_key_identity_change --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_encrypts_direct_pre_key_then_session_messages --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_encrypts_direct_pre_key_then_session_messages --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise provider_state_store_rejects_runtime_session_use_when_identity_mismatches --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise provider_state_store_rejects_runtime_session_use_when_identity_mismatches --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise provider_state_store_validates_session_records --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise provider_state_store_validates_session_records --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The store-backed group sender-key corrupt-record distribution recovery slice
added after the unusable-provider-session fallback work makes inbound
sender-key decrypt rebuild an unreadable provider sender-key record from the
stored sender-key distribution before decrypting. Valid provider sender-key
records keep the existing path, while opaque or malformed provider records are
replaced only when a valid distribution is available. The regression corrupts
the receiver provider sender-key record after storing the distribution, then
verifies high-level group decrypt recovers the plaintext and persists a valid
sender-key record. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_recovers_corrupt_record_from_distribution --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_recovers_corrupt_record_from_distribution --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_processes --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_processes --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise provider_state_store_processes_sender_key_distribution_record --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise provider_state_store_processes_sender_key_distribution_record --quiet`

- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The store-backed local group sender-key distribution recovery slice added after
the corrupt inbound sender-key recovery work makes local
`load_or_create_sender_key_distribution_record` recreate a sender-key
distribution when an existing provider sender-key record is unusable for
outbound group sends. Opaque/malformed local records and decodable sender-key
records without a local signing private key are treated as absent; valid local
records still load without rotating. The regression covers both unusable forms
and verifies the recreated record is persisted with a private signing key. It
was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise provider_state_store_recreates_unusable_local_sender_key_distribution_record --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise provider_state_store_recreates_unusable_local_sender_key_distribution_record --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise provider_state_store_loads_or_creates_local_sender_key_distribution_record --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise provider_state_store_loads_or_creates_local_sender_key_distribution_record --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_encrypts_group_relay_from_codec --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_encrypts_group_relay_from_codec --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise sender_key --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise sender_key --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The store-backed sender-key distribution persistence-order slice added after
the local distribution recovery work makes high-level
`process_sender_key_distribution` process the distribution through the active
Signal provider before storing the repository copy used by later decrypt
recovery. Malformed distribution bytes now leave both the repository
distribution namespace and provider sender-key record untouched. The regression
drives a malformed distribution through the store-backed codec and verifies no
raw bad distribution is persisted after the provider rejects it. It was verified
in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_does_not_persist_rejected_distribution --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_does_not_persist_rejected_distribution --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_processes --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_processes --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise sender_key --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise sender_key --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The PN/LID mapped session assertion send-path slice added after the
sender-key distribution persistence-order work keeps relay recipient JIDs
unchanged while mirroring a successfully queried mapped-LID session bundle back
onto the original missing PN relay address. This closes the direct/status send
case where `assert_sessions` queried `lid@lid` from the mapping store, but
store-backed Signal encryption still addressed the PN participant and therefore
could not find the freshly fetched bundle. The direct send regression uses a PN
relay recipient, a stored PN-to-LID mapping, and an encrypt query response for
the mapped LID address, then verifies the PN relay encrypts as `pkmsg` and both
the mapped LID descriptor session and PN descriptor/provider sessions exist. It
was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_with_signal_provider_mirrors_mapped_lid_session_to_pn_recipient --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise assert_sessions_fetches_missing_sessions_and_persists_them --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_with_signal_provider_fetches_session_and_writes_pre_key_ciphertext --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise assert_sessions --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_with_signal_provider --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_status_text_with_signal_provider --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The PN/LID mapped existing-session reuse slice added after the mapped send
assertion work makes non-forced `assert_sessions` mirror an already-valid
mapped-LID descriptor or provider session onto the missing original PN relay
address before deciding to query the server. This prevents direct/status/retry
send paths from issuing unnecessary key-bundle requests, and keeps store-backed
Signal encryption aligned with the relay recipient JID when the only valid
state was already stored under the mapped LID address. The regressions cover
existing mapped descriptor sessions, existing mapped provider sessions, and the
previous mapped-LID query-response path. It was verified in the current VM
with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise assert_sessions_reuses_existing_mapped_lid_descriptor_session_for_pn_recipient --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise assert_sessions_reuses_existing_mapped_lid_provider_session_for_pn_recipient --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_with_signal_provider_mirrors_mapped_lid_session_to_pn_recipient --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise assert_sessions --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_with_signal_provider --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_status_text_with_signal_provider --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The PN/LID mapped retry-session snapshot slice added after the mapped
existing-session reuse work makes `retry_session_snapshot` inspect the mapped
LID signal address when the original PN participant has no usable descriptor or
provider session. Snapshots still prefer validated provider sessions over
descriptor sessions and still report the normalized Signal address where the
session was found, so retry base-key collision tracking remains address-stable
while avoiding unnecessary refreshes for PN retry receipts whose valid session
is already stored under the mapped LID. The regressions cover mapped LID
descriptor and provider sessions for a PN retry participant. It was verified in
the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise retry_session_snapshot_uses_mapped_lid_descriptor_session_for_pn_participant --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise retry_session_snapshot_uses_mapped_lid_provider_session_for_pn_participant --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise retry_session_snapshot --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise assert_sessions --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_with_signal_provider_mirrors_mapped_lid_session_to_pn_recipient --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The business notification receive-event foundation slice added after the
multi-child dirty notification fuzz coverage work introduced a generic
`BusinessNotificationEvent` and receive-side mapping for business profile,
catalog, product, collection, order, and cover-photo notification child nodes.
The mapper preserves stanza metadata, direct child attrs/text/bytes, and bounded
nested child text/attrs so missing business notification flows are no longer
silently dropped. This is still a foundation, not full business notification
parity. It was verified in the current VM with:

The business notification EventBatch buffering slice added after the receive
event foundation work moved business notification updates through
`EventBatch`/`EventBuffer`, added a stable
`business_notification_event_store_key`, and coalesces repeated updates for the
same notification child before flush. This prepares the new surface for the
same batched/persisted receive pipeline used by other state events. It was
verified in the current VM with:

buffering gave business notification updates a native stored-record codec, a

The business media validation slice added after business notification store
persistence tightened product-image and cover-photo media boundaries. Business
media URLs now require HTTPS, a non-empty host, and no ASCII whitespace/control
bytes; uploaded-media direct paths must start with `/`; and fallback hosts are
validated before product/cover-photo helpers turn upload locations into public
URLs. The product mutation builder also revalidates public `BusinessProductImage`
struct values so callers cannot bypass URL checks by constructing the public
type directly. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core business_media --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core rejects_invalid_business_media_urls --quiet`

The Signal pre-key whisper invalid-wire fixture slice added after the

manifest with public fixture vectors for short outer base keys, short nested
inner ratchet keys, and missing inner whisper messages. The shared `wa-testkit`

The Signal pre-key whisper identity-key-length fixture slice added after the
pre-key whisper invalid-wire fixture work broadened the deterministic Signal
manifest with a public fixture vector for a short outer identity key while the
base key and nested ratchet key remain valid and matched. The shared
`wa-testkit` fixture harness now asserts malformed pre-key whisper identity
fields fail through `encode_signal_pre_key_whisper_message` with the stable
public-key length error, and the core unit test pins both encode-side and
decode-side rejection through the public pre-key whisper APIs. It was verified
in the current VM with:

The Signal pre-key whisper missing-inner-ciphertext fixture slice added after
the identity-key-length fixture work broadened the deterministic Signal
manifest with a public malformed-wire vector whose outer base key, identity
key, and nested ratchet key are valid but whose nested whisper ciphertext is
absent. The shared `wa-testkit` fixture harness now asserts that frame fails
through `decode_signal_pre_key_whisper_message` with the stable nested whisper
ciphertext error, and the core unit test pins the same decoder path. It was
verified in the current VM with:

The Signal pre-key whisper missing-inner-message validation slice added after
the missing-inner-ciphertext fixture work tightened malformed pre-key whisper
decoding so absent nested whisper payloads fail with an explicit stable
protocol error instead of falling through to a zero-length nested ratchet-key
error. The deterministic Signal manifest expectation now pins that explicit
error, and the core unit test covers the same decoder path. It was verified in
the current VM with:

The Signal pre-key session one-time pre-key state mismatch fixture slice added
after the pre-key whisper invalid-wire fixture work broadened the deterministic
Signal manifest with a generated pre-key session message that carries a
one-time pre-key id while the receive-side decrypt call supplies no local
one-time pre-key. The shared `wa-testkit` fixture harness now pins the generated
wire bytes and asserts inbound pre-key decrypt rejects the message through
`decrypt_signal_inbound_pre_key_session_message` with a stable state-mismatch
error. It also adds a focused core unit test for the same validation branch. It
was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-testkit --test signal_fixtures signal_fixtures_match_public_signal_primitives --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise signal_inbound_pre_key_session_rejects_one_time_pre_key_state_mismatch --quiet`

The Signal pre-key session unexpected one-time pre-key state mismatch fixture
slice added after the missing-local one-time pre-key state mismatch work
broadened the deterministic Signal manifest with a signed-pre-key-only pre-key
session message while the receive-side decrypt call supplies an unexpected
local one-time pre-key. The shared `wa-testkit` fixture harness now pins the
generated wire bytes and asserts inbound pre-key decrypt rejects the message
through `decrypt_signal_inbound_pre_key_session_message` with the same stable
state-mismatch error. It also adds a focused core unit test for the inverse
validation branch. It was verified in the current VM with:

The Signal pre-key session signed-pre-key signature-length fixture slice added

The status/broadcast audience validation slice added after expanded binary-node
fixtures validates status send targets before device discovery. Status sends now
accept user/LID/hosted user JIDs as audience inputs and reject group,
`status@broadcast`, device-scoped, empty, or malformed targets locally,
preventing malformed USync device queries or relay stanzas for non-user
audiences. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_status_text_rejects_non_user_targets_before_device_lookup --quiet`

The history sync event-scanner dedupe slice added after status/broadcast
audience validation deduplicates extracted history sync notifications across
top-level `MessagesUpsert` events and nested `EventBatch.messages_upsert`
events before inline processing or external download. This prevents the same
decoded notification from being applied, persisted, and emitted twice when both
receive event shapes are handed to the scanner. It was verified in the current
VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise history_sync_event_scanner_deduplicates_notifications_across_event_shapes --quiet`

- `git diff --check`

The stored media retry event replay slice added after the live e2e harness work
gives `wa-client` a restart-safe `handle_stored_media_retry_events` facade. It
pages persisted `MediaRetryEvent` records from the native store, stages matching
persisted pending-media descriptors through the existing coordinator, downloads
successful retries, removes completed pending descriptors, and only deletes
stored retry events whose pending media was actually consumed. Failed retry
events and retry events without pending media remain queued for a later pass.
The regression covers one successful stored retry, one failed retry with a
persisted pending descriptor, and one stored retry event that is still waiting
for pending media. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise stored_media_retry_events_replay_persisted_pending_media --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise media_retry --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The server-sync app-state snapshot recovery slice added after stored media
retry replay gives `wa-client` a transfer-aware
`handle_server_sync_notification_with_snapshot_recovery` facade and routes the
media-enabled incoming-node processors through it. Server-sync notifications
that return external app-state snapshots can now download and apply those
snapshots during receive-side processing instead of leaving only a pending
snapshot marker for a later manual recovery pass. The existing no-transfer
server-sync handler remains available for inline patch sync. The regression
drives a server-sync notification through `process_incoming_node_with_media_retry`,
returns an external snapshot reference from the app-state sync IQ, downloads it
through the configured media transfer, applies the decoded quick-reply mutation,
clears blocked state, and emits the recovered batch. It was verified in the
current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise process_incoming_node_with_media_retry_recovers_server_sync_snapshot --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise server_sync --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise sync_recover_and_apply_app_state_until_current --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise process_incoming_node_with_media_retry --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The receive-side media retry event cleanup slice added after server-sync
app-state snapshot recovery removes successfully consumed `MediaRetryEvent`
records from the native store when a persisted or receive-buffered media retry
batch downloads its pending media. Failed retries and retry events that still
have no pending media remain stored for later replay, preserving the restart
handoff behavior while preventing normal live processing from accumulating stale
successful retry records. The regression extends the live incoming media-retry
processor test to verify that the event store namespace is cleared after the
download succeeds, and re-runs the stored replay test to prove failed/no-pending
records are still retained. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise process_incoming_node_with_media_retry_downloads_pending_media --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise stored_media_retry_events_replay_persisted_pending_media --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise media_retry --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The spawned media retry startup replay slice added after receive-side media
retry event cleanup runs `handle_stored_media_retry_events` once when
media-enabled incoming processors start. Stored media retry responses with
persisted pending descriptors can now complete automatically after process
restart, and successful startup replays emit the same `MediaRetryProcessed`
event used by live retry notifications. The regression seeds a persisted
pending-media descriptor and stored media retry event before spawning
`spawn_incoming_processor_with_media_retry`, then verifies the startup replay
downloads the refreshed media and removes both pending and retry-event records
without requiring a new raw node. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise incoming_processor_with_media_retry_replays_stored_events_on_start --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise incoming_processor_with_media_retry --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise incoming_processor_with_placeholder_retry_and_media_retry --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise media_retry --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The media retry direct-event processing slice added after spawned startup replay
makes the client media retry helpers consume both `Event::Batch` media-retry
entries and standalone `Event::MediaRetry` updates. This matches the native
event model, where receive buffering and persistence can represent media retry
updates either as batch fields or as direct event variants. The persisted path
uses the same pending-descriptor staging and successful-record cleanup semantics
for direct media-retry events as for batches. The regressions cover non-persisted
`Event::MediaRetry` downloads, persisted direct-event staging from the native
store, successful pending/media-retry record cleanup, and the existing broader
media retry suite. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise media_retry_event_variant_downloads_pending_media --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise persisted_media_retry_event_variant_loads_store_and_cleans_success --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise media_retry --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The app-state blocked-store-key restart resync slice added after media retry
direct-event processing adds a core scan for blocked app-state collections whose
sync keys are already present in the auth store, plus client facades to retry
those collections with the regular store-key path or the snapshot-recovery path.
This covers crash/restart cases where a key-share was persisted but the live
key-share handler did not run or did not complete the resync before shutdown.
The regression first blocks a collection on a missing sync key, saves the key
directly to the shared store, rebuilds a client from that store, and verifies the
restart resync emits the quick-reply patch, advances stored app-state state, and
clears the blocked marker without another key-share event. It was verified in
the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise sync_blocked_app_state_collections_retries_after_key_is_already_stored --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise sync_with_store_keys_blocks_missing_key_then_retries_after_key_arrives --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise key_share_snapshot_recovery_downloads_snapshot_after_unblocking_collection --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The group invite notification message-stub parity slice added after app-state
blocked-store-key restart resync maps parsed group participant-invite,
participant-accept, and invite-link revoke notifications into durable
`MessageEvent` stubs. Participant invite/accept updates now use their specific
protobuf stub types instead of being dropped or collapsed into generic invite
link changes, while invite-link revoke keeps the existing
`GroupChangeInviteLink` shape with invite-code parameters when present. The core
regression drives protocol-shaped notification nodes through group update
parsing and stub generation; the client regression verifies the derived message
stubs are persisted through the native receive store path. It was verified in
the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise maps_group_updates_to_group_notification_message_stubs --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise receive_persistence_generates_group_notification_message_stub --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise group_notification --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The pending group join-request notification message-stub slice added after the
invite notification stub work maps parsed `membership_approval_request(s)`
updates into durable `GroupMembershipJoinApprovalRequest` message stubs. This
keeps ordinary pending join-request notifications distinct from the previously
covered non-admin-add lifecycle stubs while reusing the same participant JSON
parameter shape and request-method projection. The core regression drives a
protocol-shaped pending join-request notification through parsing and stub
generation, and the client regression verifies the derived stub is persisted by
the receive store path next to the existing lifecycle and invite stubs. It was
verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise maps_group_membership_request_lifecycle_to_message_stubs --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise receive_persistence_generates_group_notification_message_stub --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise group_notification --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The group notification parser/stub fuzz-target slice added after the pending
join-request stub work registers `group_notification_parse` in the fuzz
workspace. The target drives both raw decoded binary notification nodes and
structured protocol-shaped `w:gp2` group notification forms through inbound
notification parsing, group-update mapping, event-batch creation, and derived
message-stub generation. This broadens the test-parity surface around group
notification edge cases without guessing at unsupported join-request action
stub semantics. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin group_notification_parse --quiet`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo clippy --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin group_notification_parse -- -D warnings`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bins --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise group_notification --quiet`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The newsletter reaction/view notification fallback slice added after the group
notification fuzz-target work hardens receive-side newsletter update parsing for
alternate notification forms. Reaction and view children now accept
`server_id` as a fallback message identifier when `message_id` is absent;
reaction updates also accept the send-side nested `reaction code="..."` shape,
and view updates accept attribute-carried counts before falling back to node
text. The focused parser regression keeps the original text forms and adds the
`server_id`/attribute forms so the notification mapping path does not drop
newsletter engagement updates that arrive with send-side-style identifiers. It
was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise maps_newsletter_reaction_and_view_notifications_to_typed_events --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise processes_newsletter_reaction_and_view_notification_to_typed_events_and_ack --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise newsletter --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The newsletter linked-profile notification edge-case slice added after the
reaction/view fallback work broadens receive-side linked-profile mapping
compatibility while preserving the existing additive mapping model. The parser
now accepts nested `updates` arrays inside the `xwa2_notify_linked_profiles`
payload, `lid`/`lid_jid` aliases for the LID side of a mapping, camel-case
`addedProfiles`, and `phone_number`/`phoneNumber` aliases for profile PN JIDs.
All values still pass through the existing LID/PN JID validation and
normalization path before being emitted as `LidMappingEvent`s or persisted by
the client receive path. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise parses_linked_profile_notifications --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise maps_newsletter_linked_profile_notifications_to_lid_mapping_events --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise process_incoming_node_persists_linked_profile_mappings --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise incoming_processor_persists_linked_profile_mappings --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The newsletter MEX notification payload-shape slice added after the
linked-profile edge-case work broadens the existing receive-side settings and
admin-promotion paths without introducing new participant semantics. Settings
and admin-promotion notifications now accept nested `data` payloads keyed by
`xwa2_newsletter_update`, `NotificationNewsletterUpdate`, or
`NotificationNewsletterAdminPromote`, infer the operation from those payload
keys when `operation`/`op_name` is absent, and accept newsletter/user aliases
such as `newsletter_id`, `newsletterId`, `participant_jid`, and
`participantJid`. The receive mapping test covers the nested promotion form so
the parser output still becomes a typed `NewsletterParticipantsUpdate`. It was
verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise parses_newsletter_mex_update_notifications --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise maps_newsletter_mex_settings_and_admin_notifications_to_typed_events --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise processes_newsletter_mex_settings_notification_to_typed_event_and_ack --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise processes_newsletter_mex_admin_promote_notification_to_typed_event_and_ack --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise newsletter --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The inbound presence receive/persistence slice added after the account/call
notification fuzz-target work wires Baileys-style `presence` stanzas into a new
typed `PresenceEvent`, `Event::PresenceUpdate`, and `EventBatch.presence_update`
surface. Presence parsing now accepts direct `type` attributes and child-tag
fallback forms, preserves participant/timestamp/last-seen and metadata fields,
ignores local subscribe requests, routes inbound presence through
`process_inbound_node` without forcing an ACK on id-less stanzas, persists
presence updates through the native receive store, and extends the
`account_call_notification_parse` fuzz target with structured presence stanzas.
It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise maps_presence_nodes_to_typed_events --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise processes_presence_node_to_typed_event_without_ack --quiet`

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise coalesces_chat_contact_label_quick_reply_and_misc_events --quiet`

- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`

The inbound chat-state receive slice added after the inbound presence
receive/persistence work extends the same typed `PresenceEvent` surface to
Baileys-style `chatstate` stanzas. The receive router now treats `chatstate`
like presence without forcing ACKs, maps child `composing`, audio `composing`
(`recording`), and `paused` forms into presence updates, preserves target and
child metadata fields, and extends `account_call_notification_parse` with
structured chat-state stanzas. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise maps_chatstate_nodes_to_typed_presence_events --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise processes_chatstate_node_to_typed_presence_event_without_ack --quiet`

The store-backed one-to-one Signal previous-chain multi-skip slice added after
the inbound chat-state receive work broadens provider-ratchet compatibility
evidence for a remaining Signal edge case. The regression now drives a persisted
session through an inbound pre-key message, two old-ratchet session messages, a
local reply, a new remote DH ratchet with `previous_counter=3`, and then
consumes both late previous-chain skipped message keys across freshly recreated
provider facades before decrypting the next current-ratchet message. This
proves the native provider can persist and consume multiple previous-chain
skipped keys without rewinding the active current ratchet. It was verified in
the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_persists_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_persists_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_session_after_failed_new_ratchet_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_session_after_failed_new_ratchet_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The deterministic Signal previous-chain multi-skip fixture slice added after
the store-backed runtime regression promotes that ratchet edge case into the
public `wa-testkit` Signal manifest. The existing
`provider_session_previous_chain_replay` vector shape now accepts optional
second late previous-chain and next current-ratchet expectations, and the new
checked-in vector pins the generated wire messages plus provider-session record
bytes after the new ratchet, after each late old-chain message is consumed, and
after the next current-ratchet decrypt. This gives the project-owned provider
one more fixture-backed compatibility point for the remaining Signal provider
parity gap. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-testkit --test signal_fixtures signal_fixtures_match_public_signal_primitives --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-testkit --test signal_fixtures --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-testkit --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-testkit --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal stateful-record previous-chain fuzz slice added after the
deterministic fixture work extends `signal_stateful_records` with a structured
provider-session path for the same ratchet class. In addition to arbitrary
record/message decode and decrypt attempts, the fuzz target now builds bounded
valid one-to-one provider sessions from fuzzed key material, decrypts a new
remote DH ratchet first, consumes late previous-chain skipped messages, and
then decrypts the next current-ratchet message while checking the resulting
provider record remains decodable. This broadens fuzz/property pressure around
the project-owned Signal provider's remaining one-to-one ratchet parity gap. It
was verified in the current VM with:

- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records --quiet`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo clippy --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records -- -D warnings`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bins --quiet`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo clippy --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bins -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal stateful-record sender-key fuzz slice extends the same
`signal_stateful_records` target with a structured group sender-key replay
path. The target now creates a local sender-key state from fuzzed signing and
chain material, encrypts a bounded sequence of valid group payloads, builds a
matching receiver distribution record, decrypts a later message first to force
skipped sender-message-key storage, then consumes an earlier message and
decodes the advanced receiver record. This adds fuzz/property pressure around
the group sender-key out-of-order class covered by the remaining Signal parity
gap. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records --quiet`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo clippy --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records -- -D warnings`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bins --quiet`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo clippy --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bins -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise signal_sender_key_record_decrypts_out_of_order_with_skipped_keys --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise signal_sender_key_record_decrypts_out_of_order_with_skipped_keys --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_decrypts_out_of_order_group_payloads_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_decrypts_out_of_order_group_payloads_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The status/broadcast PN alias normalization slice added after the Signal
stateful-record sender-key fuzz work closes another send-path edge case. Shared
USync device extraction now canonicalizes legacy `c.us` device result JIDs to
`s.whatsapp.net`, while status audience construction normalizes `c.us` caller
targets before building the device query. The client regression covers a caller
passing both `111@c.us` and `111@s.whatsapp.net` and a USync response returning
both forms, proving the send emits one canonical
`111@s.whatsapp.net` participant encryption and one recipient count. It was
verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise normalizes_c_us_device_results_to_s_whatsapp_net --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_status_text_normalizes_c_us_targets_and_device_results --quiet`

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise classifies_relay_recipients_from_discovered_devices --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_status_text --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`

- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The direct-send PN alias normalization slice added after the status/broadcast
alias work extends the same canonical-address treatment to ordinary direct
messages. High-level `relay_proto_message` now normalizes the remote JID once
before device discovery, session assertion, reporting/tc-token handling, and
the final message stanza, while lower-level caller-supplied device relay APIs
remain unchanged. The client regression sends to `123@c.us`, verifies the USync
device query uses `123@s.whatsapp.net`, accepts a duplicated `c.us` plus
`s.whatsapp.net` device result, attaches the stored canonical tc-token, and
emits a single canonical participant encryption to `123@s.whatsapp.net`. It was
verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_normalizes_legacy_c_us_remote_before_lookup_and_relay --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text_discovers_devices_and_wraps_own_devices --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise relay_options_attach_tc_token_for_legacy_cus_target --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_text --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_status_text --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise normalizes_c_us_device_results_to_s_whatsapp_net --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The group sender-key distribution PN alias normalization slice added after the
direct-send alias work applies canonical lookup handling to participant-derived
distribution recipients. Group metadata participants, `phone_number` aliases,
and LID aliases are normalized before the distribution USync device query, so a
participant advertised as both `111@c.us` and `111@s.whatsapp.net` only creates
one canonical PN lookup and one distribution encryption. The regression also
feeds duplicated `c.us` plus `s.whatsapp.net` device results back from USync and
proves the sender-key distribution relay emits one canonical
`111@s.whatsapp.net` participant. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_group_sender_key_distribution_normalizes_legacy_c_us_participant_aliases --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_group_sender_key_text_with_distribution_fetches_participants_and_writes_relays --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_group_sender_key_text_with_signal_provider_encrypts_distribution_and_root --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_group_sender_key_message_with_signal_provider_encrypts_media_distribution_and_root --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise send_group_sender_key --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The retry all-device requester PN alias normalization slice added after the
group sender-key distribution alias work applies the same canonical lookup
rule to retry redistributions. `retry_resend_recipients` now normalizes the
all-device requester before the USync device query while preserving
participant-specific retry targets as exact device JIDs. The regression drives
a group retry whose primary requester is `123@c.us`, verifies the device query
uses `123@s.whatsapp.net`, feeds duplicated `c.us` plus `s.whatsapp.net` device
results back from USync, and proves both the sender-key redistribution and
cached replay use the canonical `123@s.whatsapp.net` / `123:1@s.whatsapp.net`
recipient set. It was verified in the current VM with:

The Signal session assertion PN alias normalization slice added after the retry
requester alias work canonicalizes the public `assert_sessions` boundary without
stripping device ids. `123:1@c.us` now becomes `123:1@s.whatsapp.net` before
descriptor/provider validation, mapped LID lookup, and key-bundle query
construction, while non-PN/LID/hosted session addresses keep their existing
device form. The regression covers duplicate legacy/canonical device aliases
plus a bare legacy PN alias and proves only canonical session users are queried
and persisted. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-client --features memory-store,noise assert_sessions_normalizes_legacy_c_us_device_aliases_before_query --quiet`

The group metadata participant alias parser slice added after Signal session
assertion alias work broadens sender-key recipient discovery at the metadata
boundary. Group participants now accept `phoneNumber`, `pn`, `pn_jid`, and
`pnJid` PN aliases; `lid_jid` and `lidJid` LID aliases; and
`participantUsername` username aliases while preserving the previous verbatim
alias storage behavior. The sender-key distribution regression now feeds
camel-case `phoneNumber` metadata and proves the parsed alias drives the
canonical distribution device lookup. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise parses_group_metadata --quiet`

The group notification invite-admin alias slice added after the `requestMethod`
work keeps invite and accept notification metadata aligned with Baileys alias
forms. `invite`/`accept` children now surface validated `invite_admin_pn`
metadata from `admin_pn`/`adminPn`, `author_pn`/`authorPn`, and generic
PN spellings, and preserve `invite_admin_username` from admin, author, or

admin aliases, author fallback aliases, and malformed admin PN rejection. It was

The Signal previous-chain skipped-key failure hardening slice added after the
invite-admin alias work tightens the project-owned provider-session decrypt
path. Stored one-to-one skipped message keys are now looked up without being
removed first and are only consumed after the ciphertext authenticates and
decrypts successfully, so a tampered late previous-chain message cannot consume
the valid skipped key in memory. The store-backed previous-chain regression now
attempts a tampered old-chain message, verifies the persisted provider session
is unchanged, and then decrypts the valid old-chain message. It was verified in
the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal sender-key skipped-message-key consume-after-authentication slice
added after the previous-chain provider-session hardening work applies the same
state-preservation rule to group sender-key skipped iterations. Stored
sender-message keys are now derived by lookup and removed only after the
sender-key ciphertext decrypts successfully, while the existing store-backed
failed-decrypt regression continues to prove persisted sender-key records remain
unchanged for valid-signature decrypt failures. A focused helper regression
locks the private lookup invariant directly. It was verified in the current VM
with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise signal_sender_key_stored_message_key_lookup_does_not_consume_before_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise signal_sender_key_stored_message_key_lookup_does_not_consume_before_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_sender_key_after_failed_group_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_sender_key_after_failed_group_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_decrypts_out_of_order_group_payloads_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_decrypts_out_of_order_group_payloads_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal inbound pre-key replay fallback slice added after the sender-key
consume-after-authentication work improves store-backed direct decrypt parity.
When a pre-key stanza references a consumed one-time pre-key, the provider now
checks for an existing session and matching stored identity before treating the
inner whisper message as an established-session message. This keeps true
missing-pre-key and identity-change failures unchanged while replayed pre-key
stanzas are rejected as duplicate/old Signal counters without mutating the
stored session. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_decrypts_inbound_pre_key_then_session_messages --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_decrypts_inbound_pre_key_then_session_messages --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_inbound_pre_key_when_one_time_pre_key_missing --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_inbound_pre_key_when_one_time_pre_key_missing --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_pre_key_after_inbound_identity_change --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_pre_key_after_inbound_identity_change --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal sender-key stale-record decrypt recovery slice added after the
inbound pre-key replay fallback work broadens group `skmsg` decrypt recovery
beyond corrupt provider records. Store-backed sender-key decrypt now retries
once with a persisted distribution when a well-formed existing sender-key record
fails to decrypt, allowing fresh distributions to repair stale or mismatched
provider state while leaving the normal existing-record fast path unchanged.
The regression seeds a valid stale receiver record, swaps the persisted
distribution to a fresh key id/signing key, and proves the incoming `skmsg`
decrypts and promotes the fresh state. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_recovers_stale_record_from_distribution_on_decrypt_failure --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_recovers_stale_record_from_distribution_on_decrypt_failure --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_recovers_corrupt_record_from_distribution --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_recovers_corrupt_record_from_distribution --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_processes_stale_and_replacement_distributions_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_processes_stale_and_replacement_distributions_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal stale previous-counter rollback slice added after sender-key
stale-record decrypt recovery tightens one-to-one provider ratchet validation.
On an inbound DH ratchet step, the provider now rejects a `previous_counter`
that is lower than the already advanced receiving-chain counter before deriving
or committing a new root/receiving chain. The pure-record regression proves the
same old-chain record can still decrypt the next valid old-ratchet message after
the rejection, and the store-backed regression proves the persisted provider
session bytes remain unchanged after the failed stale-ratchet decrypt. It was
verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise signal_provider_session_record_rejects_stale_previous_counter --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise signal_provider_session_record_rejects_stale_previous_counter --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_stale_previous_counter_without_mutation --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_stale_previous_counter_without_mutation --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise signal_provider_session_record_rejects_far_future_previous_counter --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise signal_provider_session_record_rejects_far_future_previous_counter --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_persists_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_persists_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_consumes_multiple_previous_chain_skipped_keys_after_ratchet --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-testkit --test signal_fixtures --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The deterministic Signal stale previous-counter fixture slice added after the
provider rollback guard extends the public `wa-testkit` Signal manifest with a
`provider_session_stale_previous_counter` vector. The vector builds an already
advanced old receiving chain, rejects a new-ratchet message whose
`previous_counter` moves backwards, then proves the same fixture record still
decrypts the next valid old-chain message and matches the expected post-decrypt
record bytes. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `cargo test -p wa-testkit --test signal_fixtures --quiet`

- `cargo check -p wa-testkit --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-testkit --tests -- -D warnings`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`

The Signal stateful stale previous-counter fuzz slice added after the
deterministic stale-counter fixture extends `signal_stateful_records` with a
structured reject-then-valid-old-chain path. Generated inputs now build an
already advanced old receiving chain, reject a new-ratchet message whose
`previous_counter` moves backwards, and then decrypt a valid message on the old
ratchet from the unchanged record bytes. It was verified in the current VM with:

- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records --quiet`

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_stale_previous_counter_without_mutation --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_rejects_stale_previous_counter_without_mutation --quiet`

- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal stateful sender-key failed-decrypt fuzz slice added after the stale
previous-counter fuzz work extends `signal_stateful_records` with an
authenticated group skipped-message preservation path. Generated inputs now
decrypt a later sender-key message to create skipped state, re-sign a truncated
earlier message so signature verification succeeds but body decrypt fails, and
then prove the original skipped message still decrypts from the same record. It
was verified in the current VM with:

- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records --quiet`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo clippy --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_sender_key_after_failed_group_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_preserves_sender_key_after_failed_group_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise signal_sender_key_stored_message_key_lookup_does_not_consume_before_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise signal_sender_key_stored_message_key_lookup_does_not_consume_before_decrypt --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_sender_key_provider_decrypts_out_of_order_group_payloads_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_sender_key_provider_decrypts_out_of_order_group_payloads_from_store --quiet`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal stateful sender-key distribution fuzz slice added after the
failed-decrypt preservation fuzz work extends `signal_stateful_records` with a
structured stale/replacement distribution path. Generated inputs now build an
existing sender-key state with private signing material plus a skipped key,
process a stale same-signer distribution and assert that the chain key,
private key, and skipped key are preserved, then process a same-key
different-signer replacement distribution and prove the replacement state can
decrypt a replacement-signed group message. It was verified in the current VM
with:

- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records --quiet`
- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo clippy --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records -- -D warnings`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise signal_sender_key_record_processes_distribution_and_preserves_matching_state --quiet`

The Signal stateful sender-key replay fuzz slice added after the sender-key
distribution fuzz work tightens the existing `signal_stateful_records`
out-of-order group path. After generated inputs decrypt a later sender-key
message to create skipped state and then consume the first skipped message, the
target now replays that consumed skipped message from the advanced record and
asserts the stable duplicate-iteration rejection. It was verified in the
current VM with:

- `CARGO_TARGET_DIR=target/fuzz-check cargo check --manifest-path fuzz/Cargo.toml --bin signal_stateful_records --quiet`

- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise signal_sender_key_record_decrypts_out_of_order_with_skipped_keys --quiet`

- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal stateful provider-session failed-decrypt fuzz slice added after the
sender-key replay fuzz work extends `signal_stateful_records` with a structured

session record and matching whisper message, reject a tampered ciphertext from
the original record bytes, then prove the same unchanged record bytes still
decrypt the valid message. It was verified in the current VM with:

The Signal stateful provider-session new-ratchet failed-decrypt fuzz slice
added after the active-chain failed-decrypt fuzz work extends

inputs now build a provider session on an old remote ratchet, derive a valid
new-ratchet whisper message with previous-chain skipping, reject a tampered

The Signal stateful provider-session previous-chain failed-decrypt fuzz slice

after the stateful new-ratchet replay fuzz work promotes the same edge case into
the checked-in compatibility manifest. `wa-testkit` now has a

with an old receiving chain, decrypts the first message from a new remote
ratchet, rejects a replay of that consumed new-ratchet message with the stable
duplicate-counter error, and still decrypts the next new-ratchet message while
pinning both wire messages and advanced provider-session records. It was

that builds a stored session with an old receiving chain, rejects a tampered

The deterministic Signal provider-session previous-chain failed-decrypt fixture
slice added after the new-ratchet failed-decrypt fixture coverage extends the
checked-in `provider_session_previous_chain_replay` vector shape with optional
tampered old-chain fields. The first previous-chain replay vector now decrypts a
new-ratchet message to create skipped keys for the old receiving chain, rejects
a tampered old-chain skipped message from the advanced record without consuming
that skipped key, then decrypts the original old-chain message and continues
through the existing record assertions. It was verified in the current VM with:

The deterministic Signal provider-session invalid-record raw-decode verifier
slice added after the previous-chain failed-decrypt fixture coverage tightens
the checked-in invalid provider-session vectors. The shared `wa-testkit` Signal
fixture harness now still asserts encode-time rejection for invalid in-memory
records, and also builds raw persisted provider-session bytes for the same
vectors so decode-time validation rejects hostile stored sessions with the same
stable errors for local-ratchet mismatch, receiving/remote-ratchet pairing,
skipped-key remote-ratchet requirements, zero skipped counters, duplicate
skipped keys, and uninitialized sending chains. The invalid-record fixture
schema now also supports optional extra skipped keys so duplicate persisted-key
invariants can be expressed without changing older vectors. The active
receiving-chain invalid skipped-key fixture now also builds raw persisted
provider-session bytes and asserts decode-time rejection for skipped counters at
or beyond the active receiving-chain counter. It was verified in the current VM
with:

- `cargo test -p wa-testkit --test signal_fixtures signal_fixtures_match_public_signal_primitives --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-testkit --test signal_fixtures --quiet`

- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

fixture harness now still asserts encode-time rejection for invalid in-memory
sender-key records, and also builds raw `SenderKeyRecordStructure` protobuf

The store-backed Signal sender-key invalid-record preservation slice added after
the deterministic sender-key invalid-record raw-decode verifier promotes those
stored-record invariants through `SignalProviderStateStore`. The new regression
stores raw invalid sender-key records for mismatched signing keys,
duplicate states, duplicate skipped iterations, and future skipped iterations,
then attempts decrypt, asserts the stable validation error, and verifies the raw
sender-key row is preserved byte-for-byte after each failed decrypt. It was

skipped-message fields. The out-of-order sender-key fixture now decrypts the
second group message to create a skipped key for the first message, signs a
truncated deterministic first-message ciphertext so signature verification

The deterministic Signal signed-pre-key-only pre-key session tamper fixture
slice added after the one-time-pre-key tamper fixture coverage extends the
checked-in `pre_key_session_message_no_one_time` vector shape with optional
tampered message fields. The no-one-time pre-key vector now mutates the inner
whisper ciphertext, verifies inbound pre-key decrypt rejects it with the
expected crypto error while no local one-time pre-key is supplied, and then
decrypts the original signed-pre-key-only pre-key message to keep the existing
sender and receiver provider-session record assertions pinned. It was verified

The Signal signed-pre-key-only pre-key replay guard slice added after the
deterministic signed-pre-key-only tamper fixture coverage broadens the
store-backed direct decrypt path. When a signed-pre-key-only pre-key stanza
arrives and the stored provider session already has the same remote ratchet
base key and matching identity, the provider now decrypts the inner whisper
through the existing session so exact replays are rejected as duplicate/old
message counters without rewriting stored state. Fresh signed-pre-key-only
pre-key bootstraps with a different base key still follow the normal inbound
pre-key path and can replace the session while preserving unrelated local
one-time pre-keys. The regression also covers a same-base signed-pre-key-only
pre-key message from a changed sender identity and proves it is rejected without
rewriting the stored session or identity. It was verified in the current VM
with:

- `cargo test -p wa-core --features noise store_signal_provider_decrypts_inbound_pre_key_without_one_time_pre_key --quiet`
- `cargo test -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise store_signal_provider_decrypts_inbound_pre_key_without_one_time_pre_key --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo check -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --quiet`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo clippy -p wa-core --no-default-features --features noise --all-targets -- -D warnings`
- `cargo fmt --all --check`
- `git diff --check`
- `test ! -e fuzz/target`

The Signal signed-pre-key-only failed pre-key decrypt preservation slice added
after the replay guard coverage broadens the store-backed failed-decrypt
regression. The direct pre-key decrypt test now also mutates a signed-pre-key-only
pre-key whisper ciphertext, verifies decrypt rejection, and proves no sender
session or identity record is persisted while the unrelated local one-time
pre-key remains available. It was verified in the current VM with:
